coredata.dc:title: Visual and ratiometric fluorescence detection of Hg2+ based on a dual-emission carbon dots-gold nanoclusters nanohybrid
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Huizhi
Dong, Jing
Duan, Junling
Waterhouse, Geoffrey I.N.
Hou, Juying
Ai, Shiyun
coredata.dc:description: A novel dual-emission carbon dots-gold nanoclusters (CDs-AuNCs) nanohybrid was fabricated and successfully used in the visual and ratiometric fluorescent detection of mercury (II) ions. Boronic acid functionalized carbon dots (B-CDs) were synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal method. Bovine serum albumin functionalized carbon dots (BSA-CDs) were then obtained by simply mixing the B-CDs with bovine serum albumin, aided by specific binding between the glycoprotein and boronic acid groups. Finally, the dual-emission nanohybrid was obtained by attaching AuNCs to the BSA-CDs template. The nanohybrid exhibited two well-resolved emission peaks at 440 nm and 655 nm under 360 nm excitation. The fluorescence of the BSA-CDs at 440 nm was insensitive to the presence of aqueous Hg2+, whereas the fluorescence of AuNCs at 655 nm could be completely quenched in the presence of Hg2+ through Hg2+-Au+ interactions. The fluorescence emission of the nanohybrid allowed sensitive and selective sensing of Hg2+ in the range of 2–15 nM, with a detection limit of 0.73 nM. Importantly, the emission color of the nanohybrid changed from red to blue with increasing Hg2+ concentration (due to corresponding changes in the I440nm and I655nm ratio), which could be clearly discerned by the naked eye.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid
Ratiometric fluorescent detection
Mercury (II) ions
Hg2+-Au+ interaction
Boronic acid-diol specific binding

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Hg2+ detection in tap water samples
Fabrication of the dual-emission CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid
Optical properties of the B-CDs, BSA-AuNCs and CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid
Selectivity of the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid for Hg2+ detection
Determination of Hg2+ in tap water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 41771342 , 21505086 , 21607096 ) and Funds of Shandong “Double Tops” Program ( SYL2017XTTD15 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of (A) synthesis of boronic acid functionalized CDs and their subsequent surface modification with BSA; (B) synthesis of the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid and the detection principle for Hg2+ sensing.

Low-resolution (A) and high-resolution TEM (B) images of the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid. (C) XRD patterns for the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid. (D) FT-IR spectra of B-CD (a), BSA-AuNCs (b) and the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid (c).
XPS survey spectrum for the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid (A), high-resolution C 1s (B), B 1s (C), and Au 4f (D) spectra for the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra for B-CDs, BSA-AuNCs and the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid. (B) PL emission spectra for B-CDs, BSA-AuNCs and the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid under 360 nm excitation. (C) Absorption spectrum of BSA-AuNCs and emission spectrum of B-CDs under 360 nm excitation. (D) The fluorescence lifetime of the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid in aqueous solution.

(A) Ratiometric fluorescence spectra for the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid upon addition of different concentrations of Hg2+ (0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 15, 20 nM) in PBS buffer (10 mM) at pH 8.0. (B) Plot of I655/I440 versus Hg2+ concentration in the range 2–15 nM. The plot shows a strong linear relationship (C) Digital photographs of the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid at different concentrations of Hg2+ under 360 nm excitation.

head.ce:title: Visual and ratiometric fluorescence detection of Hg2+ based on a dual-emission carbon dots-gold nanoclusters nanohybrid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University, Taian, Shandong 271018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, The University of Auckland, Auckland 1142, New Zealand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel dual-emission carbon dots-gold nanoclusters (CDs-AuNCs) nanohybrid was fabricated and successfully used in the visual and ratiometric fluorescent detection of mercury (II) ions. Boronic acid functionalized carbon dots (B-CDs) were synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal method. Bovine serum albumin functionalized carbon dots (BSA-CDs) were then obtained by simply mixing the B-CDs with bovine serum albumin, aided by specific binding between the glycoprotein and boronic acid groups. Finally, the dual-emission nanohybrid was obtained by attaching AuNCs to the BSA-CDs template. The nanohybrid exhibited two well-resolved emission peaks at 440 nm and 655 nm under 360 nm excitation. The fluorescence of the BSA-CDs at 440 nm was insensitive to the presence of aqueous Hg2+, whereas the fluorescence of AuNCs at 655 nm could be completely quenched in the presence of Hg2+ through Hg2+-Au+ interactions. The fluorescence emission of the nanohybrid allowed sensitive and selective sensing of Hg2+ in the range of 2–15 nM, with a detection limit of 0.73 nM. Importantly, the emission color of the nanohybrid changed from red to blue with increasing Hg2+ concentration (due to corresponding changes in the I440nm and I655nm ratio), which could be clearly discerned by the naked eye.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury is an environmental pollutant and deleterious to human health due to its hypertoxicity even at ultratrace levels. Amongst its various forms, divalent mercury ions (Hg2+) are widely recognized as the most detrimental pollutant that exists in the aquatic ecosystems [1]. Toxicological studies have confirmed that Hg2+ is not biodegradable and thus it has a tendency to accumulate in the human body, which can cause serious damage to the brain, kidneys, immune system and central nervous system [2]. Accordingly, it is critical to develop simple and low cost technologies that are highly selective for the detection of Hg2+ in aqueous solutions at very low concentrations.

In recent years, various analytical methods with high sensitivity have been developed for Hg2+ detection, including atomic absorption/emission spectrometry [3,4], inductive coupled plasma mass spectroscopy [5], gas chromatography-inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry [6], colorimetric and electrochemical methods [7,8]. However, most of these methods require expensive equipment and involve labor-intensive and complicated sample preparation protocols. In comparison, fluorescent detection methods are preferable since they offer high sensitivity, good selectivity, are low-cost in terms of equipment and are easy to perform requiring minimal sample preparation. Most reported fluorescent methods for Hg2+ are based on a single wavelength emission intensity change (increasing or decreasing intensity of a fluorescent probe in the presence of Hg2+). Such methods are highly susceptible to errors caused by variations in the concentration of probes, instrument factors or matrix effects in complex samples [9,10]. Ratiometric methods can eliminate all of these unwanted effects and provide a much more precise measurement of Hg2+ due their self-referencing ability resulting from the use of two emission peaks (one peak which is sensitive to the presence of Hg2+, and the other peak which is insensitive to Hg2+). The design and synthesis of dual-emission fluorescent sensors is challenging, requiring two different fluorophores in a single compact system (one fluorophore as a signal probe and the other as the reference).

Recently, fluorescent noble metal nanoclusters have attracted interest in the development of dual-emission fluorescent materials due to their unique optical properties, which include strong fluorescent intensity, a large Stokes shift, and tunable fluorescent properties [11]. Ying’s group prepared red-emissive AuNCs using bovine serum albumin as template in 2009 [12] (BSA-AuNCs), and successfully demonstrated fluorescent detection of Hg2+ [13]. Bovine serum albumin is a low cost, commercially available protein and acted as both a reducing agent and stabilizing agent in the synthesis of BSA-AuNCs. The synthetic route was economical, simple and green. On account of the strong Hg2+-Au+ metallophilic interaction, Hg2+ ions sensors based on AuNCs as the fluorescent probe possess high selectivity and sensitivity. Furthermore, the red emission of AuNCs is near ideal for the construction of dual-emission nanohybrids, allowing a wide range of blue or green emitting fluorescent materials to be used as the reference. Carbon quantum dots (CDs) possess many advantages for sensor development including facile synthesis, excellent photoluminescence, good water-solubility, easy functionalization and low cytotoxicity [14]. The main emission wavelength of CDs under 360 nm excitation is typically around 440–450 nm, significantly different to that of BSA-AuNCs (∼650 nm). Therefore, the combination of CDs and BSA-AuNCs was expected to be near ideal for the development of a dual-emission nanohybrid for Hg2+ detection.

Most synthetic methods reported towards dual-emission nanohybrids rely on a three-step procedure, independent synthesis of two kinds of luminescent materials (each with different emission characterisctics) followed by their chemical coupling [15,16]. Such 3-step processes that use a chemical coupling agent are frequently laborious and introduce a potential interferant. In this work, we developed a facile method for synthesizing a dual-emission nanohybrid by making full use of the active groups on the surface of the CDs and BSA-AuNCs, thereby avoiding the need for an additional coupling reagent. The novel nanohybrid obtained displayed a high fluorescence quantum yield and excellent sensitivity and selectivity for Hg2+ detection.

Chloroauric acid tetrahydrate (HAuCl4.4H2O) was purchased from Tianjin BASF Chemical Co., Ltd. (China). Phenylboronic acid, bovine serum albumin (BSA), NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, NH3.H2O, NaOH, Hg(Ac)2, AgNO3, NiCl2, Cu(Ac)2, CoCl2, MgCl2, MnCl2, Zn(Ac)2, CaCl2, FeCl3, Pb(NO3)2, CdCl2 and Cr(NO3)3 were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were used as-received without additional purification. Doubly deionized water was used in all experiments.

The morphology and size of the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid was analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (Tecnai G20, USA) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (Tecnai G2 F20, USA). Both instruments were operated at a lectron accelerating volatage of 200 kV. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were collected using a Bruker D8 Advance. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were obtained on a Thermo Nicolet-380 IR spectrophotometer (USA) using the KBr pellet technique over the range 400–4000 cm−1. The near surface region chemical composition of the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid was probed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI apparatus (USA) equipped with an Al Kα X-ray source (hν = 1486.7 eV). Fluorescence spectra were collected on a Cary Eclipse spectrophotometer (USA, VARIAN). UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired on a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrometer (Japan). Fluorescence quantum yields were calculated using quinine sulfate as reference, according to the method described in the Supplementary Information. Phosphorescence lifetimes were probed on an Edinburgh Instruments model FLS920 steady-state and lifetime spectrometer.

B-CDs were first synthesized according to a previous report [12,17]. Briefly, 0.2 g of phenylboronic acid was added to 20 mL of redistilled water. The pH of the solution was then adjusted to 9 by the addition of NH3.H2O, after which the solution was transferred to a 40 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting brown solution was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 30 min to remove any large dots. Finally, the solution was dialyzed against redistilled water in a dialysis membrane (2000 MW CO) for 24 h, yielding the B-CDs.

1 mL BSA (50 mg/mL) and 7 mL of the B-CDs solution were mixed thoroughly and incubated at room temperature for 24 h. Then, 1 mL of aqueous HAuCl4 (10 mM) was added under vigorous stirring. After two minutes, 0.5 mL of NaOH (1 M) was introduced and the resulting mixture incubated at 40 °C for 12 h. The resulting brown solution was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 30 min and dialyzed against redistilled water in a dialysis membrane (2000 MW CO) for 24 h, thereby isolating the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid.

The ratiometric fluorescent detection of Hg2+ was performed in phosphate buffer saline (PBS) at room temperature. Aqueous PBS (10 mM) with pH = 6.0, 7.0, 8.0, 9.0, 10.0, 11.0, 12.0 were used in all experiments. Initial experiments focused on determining the optimal pH value for the ratiometric fluorescent assay of Hg2+. A series of solutions containing 400 μL of the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid solution were prepared, buffered with PBS at pH values of 6.0–12.0. Then, an aliquot of Hg2+ of fixed concentration was added to each solution. Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were recorded before and after Hg2+ addition using 360 nm excitation, with the integrated fluorescence intensities at 440 nm and 655 nm being noted.

In a typical Hg2+ detection experiment, 400 μL CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid solution was mixed with 100 μL of Hg2+ solution of different concentrations. The solutions were then diluted to a total volume of 800 μL with the PBS (10 mM, pH 8.0), affording a series of solutions with final concentration of Hg2+ in the range of 0−20 nM (0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 15, and 20 nM). Incubation times varied from 1 to 15 min. PL emission spectra were recorded every minute, with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. In addition, photographs of the fluorescent color of the solutions were taken using a digital camera. All experiments were performed in triplicate.

To verify the selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescent probe based on the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid for Hg2+, the effect of other metal ions (Cu2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Ag+, and Cr3+) on Hg2+ quantification was investigated. A series of solutions containing the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid, PBS (10 mM, pH 8.0) single metal ions (30 nM) and Hg2+ (15 nM) were prepared. PL emission spectra were then collected under 360 nm excitation, and the PL intensity ratio (I655/I440) was obtained. In further experiments, the effect of mixed ions was also studied, in which case concentration of the individual non-Hg2+ metal ions was fixed at 15 nM. Again, PL emission spectra were collected under 360 nm excitation, and the PL intensity ratio (I655/I440) obtained. All experiments were performed in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hg2+ detection in tap water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Hg2+ determination in tap water samples was performed as follows. Tap water was obtained from our laboratory and was filtered through a membrane (0.22 μm) for further use. The filtered water samples were then spiked with a particular concentration of Hg2+, to give a series of solutions with Hg2+ concentrations of 10 nM, 20 nM and 30 nM. The CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid was then added to each of these solutions, and Hg2+ contained within the solutions quantified using the ratiometric fluorescent detection method. All experiments were performed in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the dual-emission CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of the dual-emission CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid is illustrated in Scheme 1 . Boric acid functionalized CDs (B-CDs) were synthesized via a simple hydrothermal method, resulting in CDs surface functionalized with boronic acid groups. Addition of BSA further modified the surface functionality due to specific binding between boronic acid groups and the glycoprotein (Scheme 1A). The BSA-functionalized CDs (BSA-CDs) served as a template for AuNCs synthesis. Aqueous HAuCl4 was added to a solution of the BSA-CDs, followed by incubation at 40 °C for 2 min under vigorous stirring. Finally, aqueous NaOH was added followed by incubation at 40 °C for 12 h, resulted in the formation of the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid (Scheme 1B). In the experiment, the synthesis process of AuNCs was greatly affected by pH value. As shown in Fig. S1, the AuNCs were unable to be produced in acidic condition. While adjusting the reaction pH to 10, the reduction ability of BSA molecules was greatly activated. The nanohybrid was used in the visual and ratiometric fluorescent detection of Hg2+, as shown in Scheme 1B, the fluorescence of AuNCs could be completely quenched in the presence of Hg2+ through Hg2+-Au+ interactions, whereas the fluorescence of the BSA-CDs kept stable [13]. Compared with the traditional methods used for dual-emission nanohybrid preparation, the synthetic method described here was facile, environmentally friendly (not requiring an additional coupling agent for the two fluorescent materials) and of low raw materials cost.

The structure and chemical composition of the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid was studied in detail. TEM provided valuable information about the shape and size of the nanohybrid. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the average size of nanohybrid particles was 2–3 nm, with the particle size distribution being quite monodisperse. HRTEM (Fig. 1B) revealed lattice spacings of 0.34 nm and 0.25 nm, which could be indexed to graphene (002) layers and Au (111) facets, respectively [18,19]. The HRTEM data confirms the close association of the CDs and AuNCs in the nanohybrid. The structure of the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid was further probed by powder XRD. The XRD pattern for the nanohybrid (Fig. 1C) showed a broad feature ∼22° typical for BSA [20], a sharp diffraction peak at 2θ = 23.7° corresponding to the graphitic (002) plane of the CDs [21], and four other sharp peaks at higher 2θ angles attributed to the (111), (200), (220), and (311) planes of face-centered cubic (fcc) Au [22]. Note that the Au NCs in the nanohybrid show preferential orientation, evidenced by the high intensity of the (200) reflection relative to the (111) reflection. For powder XRD on spherical Au nanoparticles, the (111) reflection is the typically the dominant reflection.

The surface composition of the as-prepared CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid was then investigated. Fig. 1D shows FT-IR spectra for the B-CDs (curve a), BSA-AuNCs (curve b) and the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid (curve c). The spectrum for the B-CDs was dominated by bands at ∼3500, 3125, 1340-1400, 1168 and 1084 cm−1, which are assigned to OH stretching (COH or H2O), OH stretching (BOH), BO stretching, BOH bending and CB stretching vibrations, respectively [17,23,24]. These characteristic absorption bands indicated that the surface of CDs were heavily functionalized with boronic acid groups. Other weaker peaks at 2940 cm−1 and 1630–1640 cm−1 are assigned to CH stretching and HOH bending or CO stretching vibrations, respectively. The FTIR spectrum for the BSA-AuNCs (curve b) was dominated by bands associated with BSA. Most prominent of these were the bands at ∼3435, 1654 and 1070 cm−1, which are readily assigned to OH or NH stretching, amide CO stretching and C-O stretching, respectively. The frequency of the amide CO stretching band is consistent with a protein containing a proportion of alpha-helices. Importantly, the characteristic SH stretching band of BSA at ∼2525 cm−1 was not observed in Fig. 1D (curve b), suggesting that the thiol groups were involved in the reduction of Au3+ to Au0 in the Au NCs synthesis (e.g. Au3+ + 3R-SH → (R-S−)3Au0 + 1.5H2), resulting in the formation of covalent bonds between the thiolate groups and the AuNCs. Following reaction between the B-CDs and the BSA-AuNCs to form the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid (curve c), the characteristic absorption band of OH stretching (BOH) at 3125 cm−1 was disappeared, confirming that the nanohybrid assembled via interaction between boronic acid groups and diols on BSA. XPS analysis provided further insight into the surface chemistry of the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid. Elements identified in the near surface region of the nanohybrid were carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, boron, sulfur, and gold. The high resolution C 1s spectrum for the nanohybrid is shown in Fig. 2 B, and was deconvoluted into four main peaks at 284.5, 285.1, 285.6, and 287.7 eV, which attributed to CC, CC, CN or CO, and amide NCO species, respectively. This interpretation is in excellent accord with the findings of the FT-IR analysis of the nanohybrid [25]. The high resolution B 1s spectrum (Fig. 2C) was deconvoluted into two peaks at 191.7 and 192.4 eV, which corresponded to BC and BO species, respectively [17]. The Au 4f spectrum (Fig. 2D) showed peaks at 84.0 and 87.7 eV in a characteristic 4:3 area ratio, which could readily be assigned to the Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2 signals, respectively. Notably, the Au 4f7/2 was divided into two distinct components at 84.0 and 84.7 eV, which was assigned to Au (0) and Au (I), respectively [12,15]. These results were well in agreement with the analysis in FT-IR spectrum and further demonstrated the formation of CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the B-CDs, BSA-AuNCs and CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the optical properties of the B-CDs, BSA-AuNCs and CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid, UV–vis absorption spectra and photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were collected. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the B-CDs had a strong absorption peak at 268 nm, which was readily ascribed to π − π* transitions of sp2-hybridized carbon [26]. The additional feature in the range 310–410 nm was assigned n − π* transitions of CO groups on the surface of B-CDs [27]. The feature extending to 500 nm was due to band gap excitation in the B-CDs (i.e. semiconductor properties of the B-CDs). The synthesized BSA-AuNCs were characterized by an absorption band at 279 nm, which was characteristic for BSA [28]. The BSA-AuNCs also showed a weak absorption maximum at around 530–550 nm, which can readily be assigned to the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) of small Au nanoparticles. The UV–vis absorbance spectrum for the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid was the weighted average of the spectra of the two components (B-CDs and BSA-AuNCs). The B-CDs, BSA-AuNCs and the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid all showed good absorption at 360 nm, indicating this wavelength would be suitable for probing their luminescent properties. Fig. 3B showed the PL emission spectrum for the B-CDs excited at 360 nm. A strong emission peak at 440 nm was observed. The BSA-AuNCs possessed a wide excitation range (Fig. S2), affording a strong fluorescence signal at 655 nm under 360 nm excitation. This suggested that the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid should show dual emission under single-wavelength excitation. As expected, the nanohybrid displayed two typical fluorescence emission bands at 440 and 655 nm under 360 nm excitation. It should be noted that the emission maximum separation of 215 nm reported here for the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid was much wider than the separations previously reported for other dual-emission nanohybrid systems [29,30]. Furthermore, the fluorescence quantum yields determined for the B-CDs and BSA-AuNCs were 10.7% and 2.3%, respectively, calculated by using quinine sulfate as reference. After combining B-CDs with BSA, the BSA-CDs were obtained with an improved quantum yield (15.0%). And subsequently, the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid were finally obtained, which reduced the CDs quantum yields (14.2%) due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) from the B-CDs to BSA-AuNCs, since the emission spectrum of B-CDs and the absorption spectrum of BSA-AuNCs overlapped (as displayed in Fig. 3C). In the meantime, the quantum yields of AuNCs were greatly improved (13.1%) in comparison to the pure BSA-AuNCs. This result suggests that synthetic strategy described here to synthesize the nanohybrid can enhance the quantum yield of the components. To better understand the excellent emission characteristics of the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid, fluorescence lifetime data was collected. Fig. S3 showed fluorescence decay curves for the B-CDs, BSA-AuNCs. The average decay time (τ average) of B-CDs (Fig. S3A) and BSA-AuNCs (Fig. S3B) was 3.52 ns and 1.88 μs, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime of CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid (τ average = 3.58 ns, Fig. 3D) was thus similar to the B-CDs. Non-radiative recombination processes were clearly dominant for the B-CDs and the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid [31].

The effects of the pH and ionic strength of solutions, and UV exposure on the PL intensity of CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid were studied to discuss the fluorescence stability of nanohybrid (Fig. S4). From Fig. S4A, we can see that the PL intensity of CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid changed less at pH 7–10. Fig. S4B showed that there was no obvious change in PL intensity at different ionic strengths. Fig. S4C indicated that CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid had an excellent photostability.

The fluorescent signal of the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid was selectively responsive to Hg2+, with its two characteristic emission peaks showing significantly different responses. As shown in Fig. S5, the nanohybrid exhibited fluorescent signals at 440 nm and 655 nm under 360 nm excitation. The fluorescence signal at 655 nm could be effectively quenched by Hg2+ addition, whereas the 440 nm signal intensity was unaffected by the presence of Hg2+. Accordingly, the nanohybrid represented an ideal platform for the ratiometric determination of Hg2+, with the BSA-AuNCs serving as the Hg2+ recognition component and the B-CDs acting as the reference fluorophore. In order to allow accurate detection of Hg2+, the effect of pH on the fluorescence of the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid and the quenching efficiency of the 655 nm peak was first studied. As shown in Fig. S4A, the maximum PL intensity in the absence of Hg2+ was obtained in pH 8.0. Further, the pH of solution markedly effected the quenched efficiency (Fig. S6). Hg2+ had the strongest effect on the PL intensity ratio (I655/I440) at pH 8.0. Therefore, buffered solutions of pH 8.0 were selected for subsequent experiments in order to achieve a lower Hg2+ detection limit.

To demonstrate the analytical performance of the ratiometric fluorescent assay for Hg2+ detection, the fluorescence response of the nanohybrid was measured upon addition of different amounts of Hg2+. Fig. 4 A showed that the fluorescence at 655 nm decreased with increasing of Hg2+ concentration, while the reference emission band at 440 nm displayed no obvious changes. Fig. S7 confirmed that the fluorescence response of the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid to Hg2+ was achieved within 3 min, and thereafter remained stable. The PL intensity ratio (I655/I440) displayed a good linear relationship with the concentration of Hg2+ in the range of 2–15 nM (Fig. 4B). The regression equation can be expressed as I655/I440 = 1.0967–0.0428c, where c is the concentration of Hg2+ in nM. The correlation coefficient was 0.9937, indicating strong linearity. The detection limit was estimated to be as 0.73 nM, far below the threshold requirement for Hg2+ detection in drinking water permitted by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (the maximum level is 10 nM) [32]. Further, the ratiometric fluorescence probe also allows visual detection of Hg2+. With increasing Hg2+ concentration, the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid showed continuous variation of fluorescence color due to the corresponding change in the I655/I440 ratio. Due to the large separation between the 440 and 640 nm emission peaks, color changes caused by a small concentration change of Hg2+ (e.g. 5 nM) could be discerned by the naked eye (Fig. 4C). Therefore, this ratiometric fluorescent assay was both very sensitive to the Hg2+ concentration and allowed visual detection of Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid for Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the selectivity of the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid for Hg2+, we systematically investigated the influence of various metal ions including Cu2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Ag+ and Cr3+ on the fluorescent detection of Hg2+. Firstly, the individual metal ions were added to solution of CDs-AuNCs. The Fig. S8A revealed that PL intensity ratio I655/I440 for the CDs-AuNCs was largely unaffected by addition of these any of these metal ions (except for Ag+, which showed a very mild quenching ability relative to Hg2+). In additional experiments, all the metal ions were mixed together to form a cocktail (with Hg2+ and without Hg2+), which was then added into solutions containing the CDs-AuNCs hybrid. Addition of the metal cocktail without Hg2+ had no impact on the I655/I440 PL intensity ratio (Fig. S8B), whereas addition of the Hg2+ containing metal cocktail dramatically lowered the I655/I440 PL intensity ratio. The decrease in the I655/I440 ratio on addition of the Hg2+-containing metal cocktail was identical to that observed for the addition of an equivalent amount of Hg2+. These results demonstrate that the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid was highly selective for Hg2+ detection, with the presence of other metal ions have negligible impact on Hg2+ quantification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Hg2+ in tap water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to gauge the wider applicability of the ratiometric fluorescent assay developed here for on-site detection of Hg2+, the standard addition method was applied to test several environmental water samples. The tap water was chosen as a real sample and was spiked with a specific concentration of Hg2+ (10, 20, and 30 nM). As shown in Table S1, the recoveries of the tap water samples were in the range 107–112%, with relative standard deviations (RSDs) < 3%. These results demonstrate that the ratiometric fluorescent assay developed here using the CDs-AuNCs hybrid was suitable for Hg2+ detection in real samples.

A dual-emission CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid suitable for the ratiometric detection of Hg2+ was successfully developed through a facile and environmentally friendly synthesis method. The nanohybrid showed strong emissions at 440 and 655 nm under 360 nm excitation. In the presence of aqueous Hg2+, the 655 nm emission was strongly suppressed, allowing accurate ratiometric detection and quantification of Hg2+. The CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid allowed the detection of Hg2+ at concentrations as low as 0.73 nM, and was extremely selective for Hg2+ even in real world samples (e.g. tap water). Finally, the CDs-AuNCs nanohybrid also permitted visible detection of Hg2+ at nanomolar concentrations due to the large separation between the 440 nm (reference) and 655 nm (detection) emissions.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 41771342, 21505086, 21607096) and Funds of Shandong “Double Tops” Program (SYL2017XTTD15).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.12.149.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene-rhodamine nanoprobe for colorimetric and fluorimetric Hg2+ ion assay
Ultratrace naked-eye colorimetric detection of Hg2+ in wastewater and serum utilizing mercury-stimulated peroxidase mimetic activity of reduced graphene oxide-PEI-Pd nanohybrids
Atomic absorption spectroscopy for mercury, automated by sequential injection and miniaturized in lab-on-valve system
Combination of diffusive gradient in a thin film probe and IC-ICP-MS for the simultaneous determination of CH3Hg+ and Hg2+ in Oxic Water
Development of a ‘collect and punch’cold vapour inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometric method for the direct determination of mercury at nanograms per litre levels
Lysine-promoted colorimetric response of gold nanoparticles: a simple assay for ultrasensitive mercury (II) detection
Oligonucleotide probes applied for sensitive enzyme-amplified electrochemical assay of mercury (II) ions
Nanomolar Hg2+ detection using β-lactoglobulin-stabilized fluorescent gold nanoclusters in beverage and biological media
A switch-on nir probe for specific detection of Hg2+ ion in aqueous medium and in mitochondria
Fluorescent metal nanoclusters: From synthesis to applications
Protein-directed synthesis of highly fluorescent gold nanoclusters
Highly selective and ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ based on fluorescence quenching of Au nanoclusters by Hg2+–Au+ interactions
Brightly near-infrared to blue emission tunable silver-carbon dot nanohybrid for sensing of ascorbic acid and construction of logic gate
Dumbbell-shaped carbon quantum dots/AuNCs nanohybrid as an efficient ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensing cadmium (II) ions and L-ascorbic acid
A dual-emission fluorescent nanocomplex of gold-cluster-decorated silica particles for live cell imaging of highly reactive oxygen species
Synthesis-modification integration: one-step fabrication of boronic acid functionalized carbon dots for fluorescent blood sugar sensing
Heterogeneous assembled nanocomplexes for ratiometric detection of highly reactive oxygen species in vitro and in vivo
Highly sensitive real-time assay of inorganic pyrophosphatase activity based on the fluorescent gold nanoclusters
Synthesis of Ag/BSA composite nanospheres from water-in-oil microemulsion using compressed CO2 as antisolvent
Preparation of graphite oxide (GO) and the thermal stability of silicone rubber/GO nanocomposites
Rational synthesis, self-assembly, and optical properties of PbS-Au heterogeneous nanostructures via preferential deposition
Infrared detection of a phenylboronic acid terminated alkane thiol monolayer on gold surfaces
Infrared spectra of phenylboronic acid (normal and deuterated) and diphenyl phenylboronate
Green and simple route toward boron doped carbon dots with significantly enhanced non-linear optical properties
Formation mechanism of carbogenic nanoparticles with dual photoluminescence emission
Broad family of carbon nanoallotropes: classification, chemistry, and applications of fullerenes, carbon dots, nanotubes, graphene, nanodiamonds, and combined superstructures
Near-infrared dual-emission quantum dots-gold nanoclusters nanohybrid via co-template synthesis for ratiometric fluorescent detection and bioimaging of ascorbic acid in vitro and in vivo
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer-based ratiometric fluorescent probe for detection of Zn2+ using a dual-emission silica-coated quantum dots mixture
Dual-emitting quantum dot nanohybrid for imaging of latent fingerprints: simultaneous identification of individuals and traffic light-type visualization of TNT
Ethylenediamine-assisted hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for sensitive biosensing and bioimaging
Nitrogen-doped photoluminescent carbon nanospheres: green, simple synthesis via hair and application as a sensor for Hg 2+ ions

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Huizhi Xie received her bachelor’s degree in material chemistry from College of Materials Science and Engineering, Shandong University of technology in 2015. She is currently a master candidate in College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University and her research interest is preparation and application of nano functional material.

Jing Dong received her master degree in polymer chemistry from College of Chemistry, Huazhong University of Science and Technology in 2004 and doctor degree in soil science from College of Resources and Environment, Shandong Agricultural University in 2013. Now she is an associate in College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agriculture University. Her current interests are eletrochemical and fluorescent biosensors.

Junling Duan is a lecturer in College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University. She received her doctor degree in Department of Chemistry of Shandong University in 2012. Her research interests include the synthesis of functional nanomaterials for the environmental pollutant analysis.

Geoffrey I.N. Waterhouse is a senior lecturer (above the Bar and at the highest step, Step 8 of Senior Lecturer grade). New Zealand follows the British system and this role is equivalent to “Associate Professor” and “Tenure” in North American universities. His current research interests is focused on polymer molecular chemistry, surface chemistry, and optical crystal engineering.

Juying Hou is a lecturer in College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University. She received her master degree in physical chemistry from Fuzhou University in 2005. She received her doctor degree in Food Science from Shandong Agricultural University in 2015. Her current research interests include preparation and application of nano functional material, new method for environmental pollutant analysis.

Shiyun Ai is a professor in College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University. He received his master degree (physics chemistry) and doctor degree (analytical chemistry) from Department of Chemistry, East China Normal University in 1995 and in 2004, respectively. His current research interests include chemically modified electrode, bioelectroanalysis, preparation and application of nano functional material, new method for environmental pollutant analysis.
